summary: Reviewed the Event Fields UI Audit against the provided pack, focusing on boundaries between the event form, detail summary/sidebar cards, event list/API surfaces, and server-action contracts. The matrix itself appears to enumerate all 44 `events` columns, but the pack does not support the stronger claim that the five read-only booking fields are the only gap across the product surface.
appears_sound: The field matrix includes every column name present in the live DB schema JSON, so no `public.events` column is obviously absent from the matrix itself.
The audit separates mutation paths cleanly by assigning main event form fields to `saveEventDraftAction` and booking operations fields to `updateBookingSettingsAction`.
`BookingSettingsCard` is rendered behind `canEdit` at `src/app/events/[eventId]/page.tsx:672`, preserving a clear edit boundary for booking settings.

findings.description: The audit’s headline claim that all event columns are accounted for in UI is broader than its stated scope, which only covers the event form, event detail page, and booking settings card. The “only gap” conclusion is not safe if event list boards, calendar views, public API responses, landing pages, or notification/email templates are part of the field visibility contract.
findings.evidence: The pack includes `src/app/events/page.tsx` composing `EventsBoard` and lists public API event routes, but provides no `EventsBoard`, calendar, public serializer, landing-page, or notification/template contents to validate.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: If the intended audit contract is strictly the edit/detail page and booking sidebar, those other surfaces are out of scope rather than missed.
findings.what_would_confirm: Add an explicit in-scope/out-of-scope section and include the list, calendar, public API, landing page, and email/notification files if they are intended to be covered.

findings.description: The audit claims 29 form fields are wired end-to-end through form, Zod, action, and database, but the pack only shows form field names. This leaves the form/action/data-access interface unverified, including camelCase to snake_case mapping and omitted/null handling.
findings.evidence: `src/components/events/event-form.tsx:711` through `src/components/events/event-form.tsx:1754` show submitted field names, but no `src/actions/events.ts` or `src/lib/validation.ts` contents are included.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The omitted action and schema files may already map and persist every listed field correctly.
findings.what_would_confirm: Include `saveEventDraftAction`, `updateBookingSettingsAction`, the relevant Zod schemas, and tests proving each submitted field persists to the expected `events` column.

findings.description: `sms_promo_enabled` has an additional admin-only UI gate inside the booking card, so the audit’s read-only-users-only gap is incomplete for non-admin editors. The architecture needs one owner for role-based visibility: either SMS promo state is admin-sensitive everywhere or it gets a read-only display for all users who can view the event.
findings.evidence: `src/app/events/[eventId]/page.tsx:672` renders `BookingSettingsCard` only when `canEdit`, while the booking card snippet renders the SMS toggle only when `userRole === "administrator"`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The product may intentionally hide promotional SMS state from every non-administrator for compliance or commercial reasons.
findings.what_would_confirm: Confirm the permission matrix for viewing versus mutating `sms_promo_enabled`, then encode that decision in `EventDetailSummary` or the booking card.

findings.description: `seo_slug` appears to be write-owned by both the Website tab and booking settings auto-generation, creating a possible drift point for public landing URLs and API links. The pack does not show whether slug generation, uniqueness, and link/cache behavior are centralized.
findings.evidence: The audit matrix lists `seo_slug` as `name="seoSlug"` in the Website tab and notes it is also auto-generated by booking settings, while `BookingSettingsCard` derives `https://l.baronspubs.com/${currentSlug}` from local slug state.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The server action may only generate a slug when none exists and may share the same canonical helper as the form save path.
findings.what_would_confirm: Review the save and booking settings actions for a single slug helper, uniqueness checks, and deterministic behavior when the form changes `seo_slug` after bookings are enabled.

findings.description: The audit’s “system-managed” category includes values that are still client-supplied as hidden form inputs, so the server boundary matters even without visible UI inputs. `eventId` and `expected_updated_at` should be treated only as identifiers/concurrency tokens, not trusted system fields to write back.
findings.evidence: `src/components/events/event-form.tsx:1750` submits `eventId` and `src/components/events/event-form.tsx:1754` submits `expected_updated_at` despite the audit grouping `id` and `updated_at` as system-managed.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Hidden form submission is normal for IDs and OCC tokens if the server action independently authorizes the user and compares the timestamp.
findings.what_would_confirm: Verify the server action authorizes by authenticated user, scopes the event by permissions, and uses `expected_updated_at` only in the optimistic concurrency predicate.

empty_categories: No confirmed circular dependency or layer import violation is visible in the included snippets.
No direct schema migration or foreign-key/index fit issue can be reviewed because the diff has no changed files and no migration contents.
No client/server bundle leak is visible in the included snippets; `BookingSettingsCard` is explicitly client-side and the detail summary snippet is display-only.
